title: Ferroelectrical Switching as a Probe of Quantum Damping in Magnetic Spin Systems
authors: Yuefei Liu, Anna Delin, Olle Eriksson, Erik Sjöqvist, Kaiyou Wang, Qirui Cui
abstract: While damped spin dynamics is important for the understanding of magnetic materials, clear signatures of \emph{quantum corrections} to the Gilbert damping mechanism remain elusive. We propose a route to distinguish quantum and classical Gilbert spin damping using ferroelectric control of a magnetic dimer. Ab initio calculations for dimers on ferroelectric substrates show that polarization reversal switches the inter-spin exchange between ferromagnetic and antiferromagnetic regimes. We formulate a magnetization-based diagnostic that relates magnetization traces to entanglement dynamics, which enables ferroelectrical on/off control of dimer entanglement. Material-informed quantum Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert simulations illustrate how the signature of magnetization dynamics can, in principle, be used to infer the existence of quantum Gilbert spin damping. This minimal and non-volatile platform connects first-principles modeling to experimentally accessible observables and provides a starting point for voltage-controlled quantum entanglement in magnetic spin networks.
summary: The research proposes using ferroelectric control over magnetic dimers to probe quantum corrections to spin damping. By linking polarization switching to the transition between FM and AFM exchange regimes, the authors demonstrate that magnetization dynamics can reveal signatures of quantum entanglement through advanced simulation techniques comparing classical and quantum descriptions.
main_problem: The primary goal is to develop a method to experimentally distinguish genuine quantum corrections from classical Gilbert damping in magnetic spin systems.
main_result: Ferroelectric polarization switching can be used to control the inter-spin exchange coupling between ferromagnetic and antiferromagnetic regimes, providing a tunable platform for studying quantum entanglement dynamics in magnetism.
method: The study combines ab initio DFT calculations to derive spin Hamiltonian parameters with time evolution simulations using both classical (LLG) and quantum (q-LLG) Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert equations.
model_or_system: The system involves magnetic dimers adsorbed on ferroelectric substrates ($	ext{In}_2	ext{Se}_3$). The magnetism is controlled by the substrate's polarization state, which modulates orbital hybridization and thus the exchange coupling ($J$) between ferromagnetic (FM) and antiferromagnetic (AFM) states.
key_observables: Magnetization traces ($	ilde{m}$), entanglement entropy ($S_{	ext{vN}}$), energy difference ($\Delta E$), and the comparison between classical LLG vs. quantum q-LLG dynamics.
important_parameters: Hubbard $U_{	ext{eff}}$, polarization state ($	ext{P}_{	ext{dn}}$ vs $	ext{P}_{	ext{up}}$), damping parameter ($\alpha = \kappa = 0.1$), and switching time intervals (e.g., 5 ps, 20 ps).
main_assumptions: The exchange rearrangement due to polarization reversal is treated as instantaneous; the DMI calculation assumes a fixed spin canting angle of $90^\circ$.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate how FE switching modulates $\Delta E$ between FM and AFM phases (Fig. 1), and show time evolution comparisons of entanglement entropy ($S_{	ext{vN}}$) and magnetization ($	ilde{m}$) between classical LLG and quantum q-LLG simulations during polarization cycling (Fig. 2).
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the physical control mechanism using DFT to link FE polarization to exchange coupling strength ($\Delta E$). It then extends this by simulating spin dynamics, comparing classical vs. quantum approaches, showing that magnetization traces carry a unique signature of entanglement-dependent dynamics only captured by the q-LLG model.
why_interesting: This work directly bridges first-principles electronic structure calculations (DFT) with open quantum system dynamics simulations (q-LLG), providing a concrete, tunable solid-state platform to observe fundamental quantum mechanical effects like entanglement in magnetic materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. The ferroelectricity tunes the magnetic dimer coupling strength from Jup to Jdn, as the In2Se3 substrate polarization switches from upward to downward, as illustrated in (a). The schematics of the crystal structure of the magnetic transition metal (TM) dimer on the In2Se3 substrate with downward polarization Pdn and upward po- larization Pup. For Cr, The out-of-plane electric polarization reaches -0.054 eÅ/u.c. in the downward state and 0.116 eÅ/u.c. in the upward state. The bonding between Cr and In2Se3 can be seen from the charge-density difference, ∆ρ = ρ(HS) −ρ(Cr) −ρ(In2Se3), as shown by the contour maps at the isovalue ∆ρ = ±2 × 10−3 e/Å3.
